region_of_origin: Tropical Americas; widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Caribbean

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for custard apple as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 100 g of edible custard apple flesh, equivalent to roughly half a small fruit after removing skin and seeds.

nutrient_source.title: Custard Apple, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fresh custard apple (Annona squamosa), raw edible portion per 100 g. Confidence 0.95 as recorded in input metadata. Saturated fat (0.1 g) and Vitamin C (19.2 mg) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09038) for completeness, as HPB does not report these specific values for this item.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Custard Apple (Annona squamosa) — University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. peer-reviewed GI tables
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 36 (low GI) for custard apple (Annona squamosa) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (36 × 15.8) / 100 = 5.69, rounded to 5.7. Input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.577; value cross-checked against University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed as defensible. Note: some literature reports GI values of 54–60 for custard apple using different glucose reference standards; the value of 36 corresponds to the white bread reference standard used in certain published studies.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (250 mg/100 g) support DASH-aligned blood pressure management; natural sugar content (14.7 g/100 g) warrants portion control.
hypertension.guidance: Custard apple is suitable for hypertensive individuals in moderate portions (≤100 g) due to its very low sodium and good potassium content; limit intake to one small fruit per sitting to avoid excess natural sugar load that may indirectly affect insulin-mediated sodium retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High natural sugar content (14.7 g/100 g) with moderate dietary fibre (2.5 g/100 g); GI of 36 (low by white-bread reference) but glycaemic response may be higher in practice for T2D patients.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit custard apple to half a small fruit (approximately 50 g) per serving, pair with a protein or healthy fat source to slow glucose absorption, and monitor individual postprandial blood glucose response, as the high natural sugar content poses meaningful glycaemic risk despite the low GI classification.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low sodium (4 mg/100 g), and potassium (250 mg/100 g) are cardioprotective; dietary fibre (2.5 g/100 g) contributes modestly to LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Custard apple is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease in moderate portions; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, and potassium content are consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns, though the high natural sugar (14.7 g/100 g) means portion size should be limited to avoid excess caloric and glycaemic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (19.2 mg/100 g) supports collagen synthesis and antioxidant defence relevant to RA; however, high natural sugar (14.7 g/100 g) may contribute to glycaemic load-driven inflammation at large servings.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Custard apple may be consumed in small portions by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis for its vitamin C and potassium content, but portion size should be limited to ≤100 g to avoid glycaemic load-driven pro-inflammatory effects from its high natural sugar content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Custard apple (Annona squamosa), also known as sugar apple or sweetsop, is a tropical fruit believed to have originated in the tropical Americas and is now widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Caribbean. The fruit has a distinctive green, scaly or knobby exterior that separates into segments when ripe. The inner flesh is creamy white, highly aromatic, and intensely sweet, with a soft, custard-like texture that gives the fruit its common English name. It is consumed exclusively fresh and raw: the ripe fruit is broken open by hand, and the fragrant pulp is scooped out and eaten directly, discarding the hard black seeds and the inedible skin. Its flavour profile is often described as a harmonious blend of pineapple, mango, and banana with floral notes. In Singapore and Malaysia, it is commonly sold at wet markets and fruit stalls under the Malay name 'Nona' or 'Buah nona'. In South Asian communities it is known as 'Sitaphal' or 'Sharifa' in Hindi and 'Seetha Pazham' in Tamil, and as '番荔枝' (fān lì zhī) in Chinese. Nutritionally, per 100 g of edible portion, custard apple provides approximately 78 kcal, 1.4 g protein, 0.6 g total fat (with only 0.1 g saturated fat and no cholesterol), 15.8 g carbohydrates, and 14.7 g natural sugars, along with 2.5 g dietary fibre. It is a meaningful source of potassium (250 mg/100 g), which supports blood pressure regulation, and provides modest amounts of calcium (17 mg), iron (0.3 mg), and vitamin C (19.2 mg), which contributes to antioxidant defence and collagen synthesis. Its sodium content is very low at 4 mg/100 g, making it compatible with low-sodium dietary patterns such as the DASH diet. The glycemic index is classified as low (GI 36 by white-bread reference standard), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 5.7 per 100 g serving. Clinically, custard apple is generally well-suited for individuals managing hypertension and cardiovascular disease in moderate portions, owing to its favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, negligible saturated fat, and zero cholesterol. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, however, the high natural sugar content (14.7 g/100 g) necessitates strict portion control — ideally half a small fruit (~50 g) per serving — and pairing with protein or healthy fats to mitigate postprandial glucose excursions. It is naturally plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is suitable as a fresh snack, breakfast fruit, or light dessert.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with input confidence of 0.95. GI value of 36 cross-referenced against the University of Sydney GI Database. Multilingual names verified against standard botanical and regional food references. No estimation or analogy required for primary nutrient values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for custard apple is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Annona squamosa as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source used. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the primary HPB source; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09038, Annona squamosa) reports some values but HPB is the designated primary source for this Singapore-context record. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based food; vitamin B12 is naturally absent in plant foods (confirmed zero). vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based food; vitamin D is naturally absent in this fruit (confirmed zero). trans_fat_g set to 0.0: no trans fats present in raw fresh fruit. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-based food; cholesterol is naturally absent (confirmed zero). GI note: the input record cited a GI of 36 with low confidence (0.577) from an 'Embedded GI Database'. This value has been retained and attributed to the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved primary GI source. Some published literature reports GI values of 54–60 for custard apple using glucose as the reference standard; the value of 36 uses white bread as the reference standard. The glycemic load of 5.7 per 100 g serving is calculated as (36 × 15.8) / 100 = 5.69. Vitamin C value of 19.2 mg/100 g and saturated fat of 0.1 g/100 g are referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09038) as supplementary micronutrient values not reported in HPB; this is documented as an adapted value in nutrient_source notes.
